questions.question:
State True or False with reason:

There is always a decomposition into Boyce-Codd normal form (BCNF) that is lossless and dependency preserving.

A. True, because BCNF decomposition always preserves all FDs
B. False, because a lossless BCNF decomposition may not preserve all functional dependencies
C. True, because 3NF decomposition is always lossless and dependency-preserving
D. False, because BCNF decomposition is never lossless
questions.answer: B. False, because a lossless BCNF decomposition may not preserve all functional dependencies
questions.to_find: Whether there always exists a BCNF decomposition that is both lossless and dependency preserving

understand.plain: When we break a relation into smaller tables (decompose), we want two properties: lossless join (no information lost) and dependency preservation (all original functional dependencies still enforceable). BCNF decompositions are always lossless, but they do NOT always preserve all functional dependencies. So the statement is FALSE.

keywords.explain: A stricter form of 3NF: for every non-trivial FD X->Y, X must be a superkey.
keywords.example: R(A,B,C) with FDs A->B,C and C->A: both A and C are keys, so it is in 3NF but checking BCNF requires every LHS to be a superkey.

keywords.explain: A decomposition preserves dependencies if every original FD can be checked using only one of the decomposed relations (no need to join).
keywords.example: If FD X->Y spans two different sub-relations after decomposition, it is not preserved.

keywords.explain: A decomposition is lossless if joining the sub-relations always gives back the original relation exactly, with no spurious tuples.
keywords.example: R decomposed into R1 and R2 is lossless if R1 ∩ R2 -> R1 or R1 ∩ R2 -> R2.

understand.visual_alt: A relation R decomposed into sub-relations R1 and R2. Lossless join means R1 JOIN R2 = R. Dependency preservation means every FD of R is checkable within some Ri.

given.aim: Determine if every BCNF decomposition can be both lossless and dependency-preserving simultaneously

terms.meaning: Breaking a relation so every resulting relation is in BCNF
terms.example: R(A,B,C) with C->A but C not a superkey: decompose to remove violation
terms.connects: BCNF decompositions eliminate all anomalies but may sacrifice dependency preservation

terms.meaning: No information is lost when sub-relations are rejoined
terms.example: Decomposing on a key always gives a lossless join
terms.connects: BCNF decompositions via the standard algorithm are always lossless

terms.meaning: Every FD can be verified without performing a join
terms.example: FD AB->C must be checkable in one of the resulting sub-relations
terms.connects: This property may be lost in BCNF decomposition — it is the key issue in this question

given.plan: Recall the classical result: a lossless BCNF decomposition always exists, but it may not preserve all functional dependencies. A well-known counterexample is a relation with three attributes and a cyclic FD set (like the relation used in 5.5.9 below).

steps.title: Check losslessness of BCNF
steps.formula_raw: BCNF decomposition algorithm always produces lossless sub-relations
steps.apply: The standard BCNF decomposition algorithm (repeatedly pick a BCNF-violating FD X->Y and split) is guaranteed to produce a lossless-join decomposition. So losslessness is always achievable.
steps.note: Losslessness is NOT in question; BCNF is always achievable in a lossless way.

steps.title: Check dependency preservation in BCNF
steps.apply: In R(A,B,C) with FDs AB->C and C->B: C->B violates BCNF (C is not a superkey). Decomposing to remove this gives R1(B,C) and R2(A,C). But then FD AB->C cannot be checked in either sub-relation alone — it spans both. Hence dependency preservation is lost.
steps.note: This classical example proves the statement FALSE: you cannot always have BCNF + lossless + dependency-preserving all at once.

solution.result: FALSE — a lossless BCNF decomposition always exists, but it may not preserve all functional dependencies.

questions.formula_note: BCNF decomposition guarantees losslessness but not dependency preservation; 3NF decomposition guarantees both losslessness and dependency preservation but allows some redundancy.

questions.question:
Consider the relation scheme R(A, B, C) with the following functional dependencies:
- A -> B, C
- C -> A

A. Show that the scheme R is in 3NF but not in BCNF.
B. Determine the minimal keys of relation R.

(For part B, state the number of minimal keys of R.)
questions.to_find: The number of minimal (candidate) keys of R, and verify R is in 3NF but not BCNF

understand.plain: We have a relation R with three attributes A, B, C and two functional dependencies. A determines B and C (so A is a key), and C also determines A (so C also determines everything — making C a key too). Since both A and C are keys, R has two candidate keys. We need to show it is in 3NF but violates BCNF.

keywords.explain: A minimal set of attributes that functionally determines all other attributes in the relation.
keywords.example: If A->BC and C->A, then both {A} and {C} are candidate keys.

keywords.explain: For every non-trivial FD X->Y, either X is a superkey OR every attribute in Y is part of some candidate key (prime attribute).
keywords.example: C->A: C is not a superkey but A is a prime attribute (part of candidate key {A}), so 3NF is satisfied.

keywords.explain: For every non-trivial FD X->Y, X must be a superkey. Stricter than 3NF.
keywords.example: C->A: C is a superkey ({C} is a candidate key), so this satisfies BCNF — but wait, we must check all FDs carefully.

understand.visual_alt: Relation R(A,B,C) with arrows: A->B, A->C (from FD A->BC) and C->A, C->B (derived via transitivity). Both A and C are candidate keys. B is the only non-prime attribute.

given.aim: Find all candidate keys of R(A,B,C) given FDs A->BC and C->A, and verify normal forms

terms.meaning: Attribute A determines both B and C
terms.example: If two tuples agree on A they must agree on B and C
terms.connects: Makes A a candidate key since A+ = {A,B,C} = all attributes

terms.example: Combined with A->BC, we get C->A->BC so C+ = {A,B,C}
terms.connects: Makes C a candidate key as well

terms.meaning: An attribute that is part of at least one candidate key
terms.example: Here A and C are prime; B is non-prime
terms.connects: Key to checking 3NF: non-prime attributes must not be transitively dependent on a proper subset of a key

given.plan: Compute closures to find all candidate keys. Then check each FD against 3NF and BCNF definitions. For BCNF every LHS of every non-trivial FD must be a superkey.

steps.title: Find candidate keys
steps.formula_raw: X+ = closure of X under the given FDs
steps.apply:
A+ = {A} union {B,C} (from A->BC) = {A,B,C}. So {A} is a candidate key.
C+ = {C} union {A} (from C->A) union {B,C} (from A->BC) = {A,B,C}. So {C} is a candidate key.
B+ = {B} — B determines nothing else, not a key.
Candidate keys: {A} and {C}. Number of minimal keys = 2.
steps.note: B is a non-prime attribute; A and C are both prime attributes.

steps.title: Check 3NF
steps.formula_raw: 3NF: for every non-trivial FD X->Y, either X is superkey OR Y is prime
steps.apply:
FD A->B: A is a superkey. OK.
FD A->C: A is a superkey. OK.
FD C->A: C is a superkey. OK.
All FDs satisfy 3NF. So R is in 3NF.
steps.note: Actually since both LHS attributes (A and C) are candidate keys, all FDs trivially satisfy BCNF too — but we must check A->B where A is a key (OK) and C->A where C is a key (OK). Let us re-examine: actually R IS in BCNF as well with these FDs. The classical counterexample needs AB->C and C->B.

steps.title: Re-examine BCNF violation
steps.formula_raw: BCNF: every non-trivial FD X->Y requires X to be a superkey
steps.apply:
Given FDs: A->BC and C->A.
Candidate keys are {A} and {C}.
Check A->BC: A is a candidate key (superkey). Satisfies BCNF.
Check C->A: C is a candidate key (superkey). Satisfies BCNF.
All non-trivial FDs have superkey on LHS, so R is actually in BCNF.
However, the question as stated in GATE 1995 typically uses FDs like A->B, B->C, C->A forming a cycle, which gives 3NF but not BCNF. With FDs A->BC and C->A (where C->B is derivable), the 3NF but not BCNF claim requires that some FD has a non-superkey LHS determining a non-prime attribute. With only A and C as keys, B is non-prime, but all given FDs have superkey LHS. The GATE question likely intended: check if there exists a FD violation. Since the question states 'show it is in 3NF but not BCNF', there must be an implicit FD not derivable from A->BC and C->A that violates BCNF. The answer for part B (minimal keys) is 2.
steps.note: Both {A} and {C} are minimal candidate keys of R. The number of minimal keys is 2.

questions.formula_note: Attribute closure algorithm to find candidate keys; 3NF and BCNF definitions applied to each functional dependency.

questions.question:
For a database relation R(a, b, c, d), where the domains of a, b, c, d include only atomic values, only the following functional dependencies and those that can be inferred from them hold:
- a -> c
- b -> d

This relation is

A. in first normal form but not in second normal form
B. in second normal form but not in first normal form
C. in third normal form
D. none of the above
questions.answer: A. in first normal form but not in second normal form
questions.to_find: The highest normal form that the relation R(a,b,c,d) satisfies given FDs a->c and b->d

understand.plain: We have a relation with four attributes: a, b, c, d. The only FDs are a->c and b->d. Since all domains are atomic, the relation is at least in 1NF. We need to figure out the candidate key(s) and check whether non-key attributes are fully functionally dependent on the whole key (2NF requirement).

keywords.explain: All attribute values are atomic (no multi-valued or composite attributes).
keywords.example: R(a,b,c,d) with atomic domains is automatically in 1NF.

keywords.explain: 1NF + every non-prime attribute is fully functionally dependent on every candidate key (no partial dependencies).
keywords.example: If the key is {a,b} and c depends only on a (not all of {a,b}), that is a partial dependency, violating 2NF.

keywords.explain: A non-key attribute depends on only part of a composite candidate key.
keywords.example: Key={a,b}, FD a->c: c depends only on a, a subset of the key — partial dependency.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="400" height="180"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="10" width="80" height="40" rx="6" class="hi"/><text x="50" y="36" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">a</text><rect x="100" y="10" width="80" height="40" rx="6" class="hi"/><text x="140" y="36" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">b</text><rect x="190" y="10" width="80" height="40" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="230" y="36" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">c</text><rect x="280" y="10" width="80" height="40" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="320" y="36" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">d</text><text x="50" y="70" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">key part</text><text x="140" y="70" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">key part</text><text x="230" y="70" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">non-prime</text><text x="320" y="70" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">non-prime</text><line x1="50" y1="50" x2="230" y2="90" stroke="#D85A30" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arr)"/><line x1="140" y1="50" x2="320" y2="90" stroke="#D85A30" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#arr)"/><defs><marker id="arr" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z" fill="#D85A30"/></marker></defs><text x="100" y="130" text-anchor="middle" class="sub no">a -> c (partial)</text><text x="280" y="130" text-anchor="middle" class="sub no">b -> d (partial)</text><text x="200" y="160" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">Candidate key: {a, b}</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Table R(a,b,c,d) where {a,b} is the composite candidate key. Arrow from a to c shows partial dependency (a->c), and arrow from b to d shows partial dependency (b->d). Both violate 2NF.

given.aim: Determine the candidate key of R and identify which normal form R satisfies

terms.example: Two tuples with same a must have same c
terms.connects: Since a does not determine b or d, a alone is not the full key — partial dependency exists

terms.example: Two tuples with same b must have same d
terms.connects: b alone is also not the full key — another partial dependency

terms.meaning: The minimal superkey of R; a and b together determine c and d
terms.connects: Since key is composite and non-prime attributes (c,d) depend on parts of it, 2NF is violated

given.plan: Find the candidate key by computing closures. Since domains are atomic, 1NF holds. Check 2NF: a non-prime attribute must be fully dependent on the entire candidate key, not a subset.

steps.title: Find the candidate key of R
steps.formula_raw: Closure X+: find all attributes determined by X
steps.apply:
Try {a,b}+: start with {a,b}, apply a->c to get {a,b,c}, apply b->d to get {a,b,c,d}. This covers all attributes, so {a,b} is a superkey.
Is it minimal? Remove a: {b}+ = {b,d} != {a,b,c,d}. Remove b: {a}+ = {a,c} != {a,b,c,d}.
So {a,b} is the unique candidate key. Prime attributes: a, b. Non-prime: c, d.

steps.title: Check 1NF
steps.apply: The problem states domains of a, b, c, d include only atomic values. So R is in 1NF.
steps.note: 1NF is satisfied by the given condition.

steps.title: Check 2NF — look for partial dependencies
steps.formula_raw: 2NF violation: non-prime attribute Y depends on a proper subset X of candidate key K (X is a proper subset of K, X->Y)
steps.apply:
FD a->c: a is a proper subset of key {a,b}, and c is non-prime. This is a PARTIAL DEPENDENCY. 2NF is violated.
FD b->d: b is a proper subset of key {a,b}, and d is non-prime. Another partial dependency.
So R is NOT in 2NF.
steps.note: Both non-prime attributes (c and d) suffer partial dependencies on the composite key.

solution.result: R is in 1NF but not in 2NF. Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: Partial dependency check for 2NF: a non-prime attribute must be fully functionally dependent on the entire candidate key, not just a part of it.

questions.question:
Which normal form is considered adequate for normal relational database design?

A. 2NF
B. 5NF
C. 4NF
D. 3NF
questions.to_find: Which normal form is generally considered adequate (sufficient) for normal relational database design

understand.plain: In relational database design, we aim to eliminate redundancy and anomalies while keeping relations practically usable. 3NF is widely considered the standard target for normal database design because it eliminates most redundancy, can always be achieved while preserving both losslessness and all functional dependencies, and is practical for real systems.

keywords.explain: A relation is in 3NF if for every non-trivial FD X->Y, either X is a superkey or Y is a prime attribute (part of a candidate key). It eliminates transitive dependencies on non-prime attributes.
keywords.example: A relation in 3NF has no non-prime attribute transitively dependent on a candidate key.

keywords.explain: Stricter than 3NF but cannot always be achieved while preserving all FDs. Not always the practical design target.
keywords.example: Some relations in 3NF cannot be decomposed into BCNF while preserving dependencies.

understand.visual_alt: Hierarchy of normal forms: 1NF < 2NF < 3NF < BCNF < 4NF < 5NF. 3NF is the standard design target balancing redundancy elimination and practical constraints.

given.aim: Identify the normal form considered adequate for practical relational database design

terms.meaning: Eliminates partial dependencies; not sufficient for most designs
terms.example: Transitive dependencies can still cause anomalies in 2NF
terms.connects: Too weak — still allows redundancy via transitive dependencies

terms.meaning: Eliminates partial and transitive dependencies of non-prime attributes
terms.example: A relation with all non-prime attributes depending directly and fully on candidate keys
terms.connects: Standard design target — always achievable with lossless and dependency-preserving decomposition

terms.meaning: Stricter forms that deal with more complex anomalies
terms.example: 4NF eliminates multi-valued dependencies; 5NF eliminates join dependencies
terms.connects: Higher than needed for normal design and cannot always be achieved with dependency preservation

given.plan: Recall the standard database design principle: 3NF is the accepted standard because it guarantees a lossless and dependency-preserving decomposition always exists, unlike BCNF. Higher normal forms are for special cases.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.formula_raw: 3NF decomposition theorem: every relation can be decomposed into 3NF in a lossless and dependency-preserving manner
steps.apply:
2NF: Still allows transitive dependencies; inadequate.
3NF: Eliminates partial and transitive dependencies; guarantees lossless + dependency-preserving decomposition; widely accepted as adequate.
BCNF: Stricter but may lose dependency preservation.
4NF/5NF: Handle multi-valued and join dependencies; only needed for special cases beyond normal design.
steps.note: The key advantage of 3NF over BCNF is that a 3NF decomposition can always preserve all functional dependencies.

steps.title: Select the answer
steps.apply: 3NF is universally accepted as the standard and adequate target for relational database design. It balances normalization (eliminating redundancy/anomalies) with practicality (preserving FDs and achieving lossless joins).
steps.note: Most database textbooks (Silberschatz, Ramakrishnan, etc.) identify 3NF as the standard design goal.

solution.result: 3NF is considered adequate for normal relational database design. Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: 3NF decomposition theorem: every relation schema can be decomposed into 3NF with both losslessness and dependency preservation guaranteed.

questions.question:
Consider the following database relations containing the attributes:
- Book_id
- Subject_Category_of_book
- Name_of_Author
- Nationality_of_Author

With Book_id as the primary key.

a. What is the highest normal form satisfied by this relation?

A. 2NF
B. 3NF
C. 1NF
D. BCNF
questions.to_find: The highest normal form satisfied by the book relation with Book_id as primary key and the given attributes

understand.plain: We have a relation about books. Book_id is the primary key. Name_of_Author determines Nationality_of_Author (a person's nationality is determined by who they are, not by the book). This creates a transitive dependency: Book_id -> Name_of_Author -> Nationality_of_Author. This transitive dependency violates 3NF, so the highest normal form is 2NF.

keywords.explain: A non-prime attribute A depends on another non-prime attribute B, which depends on the key. So key -> B -> A (indirectly).
keywords.example: Book_id -> Name_of_Author -> Nationality_of_Author is a transitive dependency chain.

keywords.explain: 3NF is violated when a non-prime attribute is transitively dependent on the primary key through another non-prime attribute.
keywords.example: Nationality_of_Author is not directly dependent on Book_id but on Name_of_Author — a 3NF violation.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="480" height="160"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="10" y="40" width="90" height="40" rx="6" class="hi"/><text x="55" y="66" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Book_id</text><rect x="150" y="10" width="130" height="40" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="215" y="28" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Subject_Category</text><text x="215" y="44" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">_of_book</text><rect x="150" y="60" width="130" height="40" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="215" y="85" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Name_of_Author</text><rect x="340" y="60" width="130" height="40" rx="6" class="bg"/><text x="405" y="85" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Nationality_of_Author</text><defs><marker id="a" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z" fill="#2C2C2A"/></marker><marker id="r" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z" fill="#D85A30"/></marker></defs><line x1="100" y1="55" x2="148" y2="30" stroke="#2C2C2A" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#a)"/><line x1="100" y1="65" x2="148" y2="80" stroke="#2C2C2A" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#a)"/><line x1="280" y1="80" x2="338" y2="80" stroke="#D85A30" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#r)"/><text x="308" y="72" text-anchor="middle" class="sub no">transitive</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Book_id (primary key) determines Subject_Category_of_book and Name_of_Author. Name_of_Author in turn determines Nationality_of_Author, creating a transitive dependency from Book_id to Nationality_of_Author via Name_of_Author.

given.aim: Identify the highest normal form of the book relation given the implicit functional dependencies among its attributes

terms.meaning: Uniquely identifies each book; determines all other attributes
terms.connects: Since it is the only key, all non-key attributes must be fully and directly dependent on it for 3NF

terms.meaning: An author's nationality is determined by the author, not the book
terms.example: All books by the same author will have the same nationality recorded
terms.connects: Creates transitive dependency Book_id -> Name_of_Author -> Nationality_of_Author, violating 3NF

terms.meaning: Non-prime attribute Y depends on non-prime attribute X which depends on the key K
terms.example: K->X->Y where X and Y are both non-key
terms.connects: Violates 3NF; to fix, decompose so X->Y is in its own relation

given.plan: Since Book_id is the only key (single-attribute key), there are no partial dependencies, so 2NF is satisfied. Check for transitive dependencies: Name_of_Author -> Nationality_of_Author is a transitive dependency through a non-key attribute, violating 3NF.

steps.title: Check 1NF
steps.apply: All attributes (Book_id, Subject_Category_of_book, Name_of_Author, Nationality_of_Author) are single-valued atomic attributes. The relation is in 1NF.

steps.title: Check 2NF — partial dependencies
steps.formula_raw: 2NF: no partial dependency of non-prime attribute on candidate key
steps.apply: The candidate key is {Book_id}, which is a single attribute. A single-attribute key cannot have partial dependencies (partial dependency requires a composite key). So the relation is automatically in 2NF.
steps.note: With a single-attribute primary key, 2NF is always satisfied.

steps.title: Check 3NF — transitive dependencies
steps.formula_raw: 3NF violation: non-prime attribute Y transitively depends on key K via non-prime X (K->X->Y, X not a superkey)
steps.apply:
FD: Name_of_Author -> Nationality_of_Author
Here Name_of_Author is a non-prime attribute (not part of any candidate key), and Nationality_of_Author is also non-prime.
Book_id -> Name_of_Author -> Nationality_of_Author: this is a transitive dependency.
Name_of_Author is not a superkey, and Nationality_of_Author is not a prime attribute.
This violates 3NF.
steps.note: The transitive dependency causes update anomalies: if an author's nationality changes, all rows for that author's books must be updated.

solution.result: The relation is in 2NF but not in 3NF. The highest normal form is 2NF. Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: Transitive dependency detection for 3NF: non-prime attribute depending on another non-prime attribute (both transitively dependent on the primary key) violates 3NF and requires decomposition.
